title: PRACTICE: Climate optimism and international climate goals
description: This exercise is designed to check out what you’ve learned about the positive progress being made in the fight against climate change and the challenges of getting international climate policies to stick. Let’s see what you know about staying optimistic and understanding global efforts to tackle climate change!
topic_path: PRACTICE: Climate optimism and international climate goals

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is __not__ a good reason to be optimistic about the ability of humans to solve the climate crisis?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Humans will simply adapt to the natural changes brought about by climate warming.
New technological innovations are being developed to help fight climate change.
Economic actors like governments and businesses are taking climate change into account in their economic decisions.
Young people are choosing to commit their lives and education to fighting climate change.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following may best describe the role of private business and entrepreneurs in solving climate change?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Humanity has made innovations to navigate crises in the past. Given new incentives to innovate, businesses can play a key role in quickly bringing new technologies to market.
Private businesses and entrepreneurs are always looking to make things better for consumers and the planet. They are best situated to help us tackle the climate crisis.
Governments can and will mandate that businesses and entrepreneurs work to solve climate change as they innovate and create new technologies.
Businesses and entrepreneurs are not likely to prioritize innovation that helps to address climate change because they are focused on profit incentives.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following describes the technological innovations that are the most promising when it comes to the future of the climate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: We have new ways to store energy from renewable sources.
Humans have already created all the innovations that we need to make clean energy.
Electric vehicles will revolutionize the transportation sector for decades to come.
Increased usage of biodegradable materials instead of single-use plastics will help clean up the oceans.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why is the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) an important component of the fight against climate change?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It assembles hundreds of scientists, researchers and policy makers to communicate actionable knowledge about climate change with the public.
It negotiates and ratifies treaties between member-states of the United Nations.
It sets regulatory standards for greenhouse gas emissions and monitors emissions among its member states to ensure compliance.
It researches, develops, and commercializes technological solutions to climate change.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an example of a successful international agreement to address climate change?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Montreal Protocol and Kigali agreement
The CITES Agreement
The Kyoto Protocol
The IPCC

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the significance of a $1.5 \degree \text{Celsius}$ difference in terms of climate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It can have significant impacts on many populations.
It is not all that significant if you look into other eons.
It will melt all of the polar ice caps and the rising sea levels will end civilization as we know it.
It is a critical tipping point that will result in uncontrollable global warming.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which response best explains why there was massive shock and reaction to the $2018$ special report on climate change?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It warned of serious impacts even at $1.5 \degree \text{C}$ of warming.
It predicted a $90\%$ drop in biodiversity within the next $10$ years.
It predicted that the world would far surpass $1.5 \degree \text{C}$ of warming and that all climate change is extremely advanced and irreversible.

It stated that all nations of the world would need to halt industrial activities immediately in order to stave off a climate disaster and save humanity. It also announced that we needed to reach net-zero by $2025$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which response best exemplifies the challenges and progress made since the goal of $1.5 \degree \text{C}$  was set in the Paris Agreement?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: While some scientists and policymakers believe it’s still technically possible to reach the $1.5 \degree \text{C}$ goal, many more think the chances are pretty slim.
The goal of  $1.5 \degree \text{C}$ will definitely be met within our lifetime given the sheer amount of innovation that has been achieved.
Once it was deemed unattainable and unrealistic, the nations of the world gave up on keeping the rate of warming below $1.5 \degree \text{C}$.
The $1.5 \degree \text{C}$ goal has inspired countries to rapidly deindustrialize and greatly reduce their carbon emissions, leading to massive economic disruptions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why do some countries argue that they should not have restrictions on their emissions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They argue that they deserve the same opportunities the US and Europe had to develop their economies.
They argue that their economies are much smaller than the US and Europe and  should not face restrictions on their economic development.
They argue that climate change has already been solved, and that their obligations under international agreements have been met.
They argue that their historical emissions are negligible compared to the United States or Western Europe, and so they should not be held responsible.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following describes *“a climate doomer”* and their outlook on the future of the climate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Climate doomers either think fear is a more powerful motivator towards action or that climate change is too severe to reverse.
Climate doomers believe that climate change will resolve itself as there are always “natural" ebbs and flows in the earth’s climate.
Climate doomers believe that human interventions make the climate crisis worse, as new innovations and interventions cause new climate challenges.
Climate doomers are optimistic that human innovation and access to  renewable energy will help humans to thrive and push back on climate change.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How do climate “optimists” differ  from “doomers” in their approach towards addressing climate change?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Optimists believe in the ability of human beings to tackle the most challenging problems we face due to climate change.
Climate optimists assume that fear is a stronger motivator towards action or that it’s too late for human beings to take action to rectify the climate crisis.
Many climate change optimists believe that *time is on our side*, and that human beings have ample opportunities to address the challenges of climate change.
Many climate optimists believe that the natural ebbs and flows in climate over the eons prove that climate change is a part of life on earth, which will resolve itself with time.
